{{Accuracy|date=December 2014}}<!-- Editor's responsibility to provide reliable sources for newly created articles --> {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Sōma Ōta Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:相馬太田神社.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta, Azadategoshi 143, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |ISO = JP-07 |Latitude degrees = 37|Latitude minutes = 36|Latitude seconds = 39.5 |Longitude degrees = 140|Longitude minutes = 58|Longitude seconds = 3.1 |Label position = left |Map = Japan Fukushima#Japan |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} |Shrine rank = |consecration_year = Genkyō 3 (1321) |Main hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Alternate name = Myōken Ōta Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Festival = |Ceremonies = {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (July 23–25) }}
'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}.
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On {{ill|Ōmisoka|lt=New Year’s Eve|ja|大晦日}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas
== See Also == * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|de|Burg_Sōma-Nakamura}} * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

{{Accuracy|date=December 2014}}<!-- Editor's responsibility to provide reliable sources for newly created articles --> {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Sōma Ōta Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:相馬太田神社.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta, Azadategoshi 143, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |ISO = JP-07 |Latitude degrees = 37|Latitude minutes = 36|Latitude seconds = 39.5 |Longitude degrees = 140|Longitude minutes = 58|Longitude seconds = 3.1 |Label position = left |Map = Japan Fukushima#Japan |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} |Shrine rank = |consecration_year = Genkyō 3 (1321) |Main hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Alternate name = Myōken Ōta Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Festival = |Ceremonies = {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (July 23–25) }}
'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}.
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On {{ill|Ōmisoka|lt=New Year’s Eve|ja|大晦日}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas
== See Also == * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|de|Burg_Sōma-Nakamura}} * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

{{Accuracy|date=December 2014}}<!-- Editor's responsibility to provide reliable sources for newly created articles --> {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Sōma Ōta Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:相馬太田神社.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta, Azadategoshi 143, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |ISO = JP-07 |Latitude degrees = 37|Latitude minutes = 36|Latitude seconds = 39.5 |Longitude degrees = 140|Longitude minutes = 58|Longitude seconds = 3.1 |Label position = left |Map = Japan Fukushima#Japan |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} |Shrine rank = |consecration_year = Genkyō 3 (1321) |Main hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Alternate name = Myōken Ōta Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Festival = |Ceremonies = {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (July 23–25) }}
'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}.
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On {{ill|Ōmisoka|lt=New Year’s Eve|ja|大晦日}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|de|Burg_Sōma-Nakamura}} * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

On {{ill|Ōmisoka|lt=New Year’s Eve|ja|大晦日}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|de|Burg_Sōma-Nakamura}} * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)}}

{{Accuracy|date=December 2014}}<!-- Editor's responsibility to provide reliable sources for newly created articles --> {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Sōma Ōta Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:相馬太田神社.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta, Azadategoshi 143, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |ISO = JP-07 |Latitude degrees = 37|Latitude minutes = 36|Latitude seconds = 39.5 |Longitude degrees = 140|Longitude minutes = 58|Longitude seconds = 3.1 |Label position = left |Map = Japan Fukushima#Japan |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} |Shrine rank = |consecration_year = Genkyō 3 (1321) |Main hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Alternate name = Myōken Ōta Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Festival = |Ceremonies = {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} (July 23–25) }}
'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}.
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On {{ill|Ōmisoka|lt=New Year’s Eve|ja|大晦日}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|de|Burg_Sōma-Nakamura}} * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|de|Burg_Sōma-Nakamura|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

{{Accuracy|date=December 2014}}<!-- Editor's responsibility to provide reliable sources for newly created articles --> {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Sōma Ōta Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:相馬太田神社.jpg|250px]] |location = {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=draft}}, Nakada Ōta, Azadategoshi 143, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県|12=draft}} |ISO = JP-07 |Latitude degrees = 37|Latitude minutes = 36|Latitude seconds = 39.5 |Longitude degrees = 140|Longitude minutes = 58|Longitude seconds = 3.1 |Label position = left |Map = Japan Fukushima#Japan |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}} |Shrine rank = |consecration_year = Genkyō 3 (1321) |Main hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} |Alternate name = Myōken Ōta Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Festival = |Ceremonies = {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} (July 23–25) }}
'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|ja|南相馬市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=draft}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}}.
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=draft}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏|12=draft}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=draft}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Ōshū Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|12=draft}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|ja|千葉氏|12=draft}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=draft}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|ja|相馬氏|12=draft}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=draft}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On {{ill|Ōmisoka|lt=New Year’s Eve|ja|大晦日|12=draft}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=draft}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|ja|中村城 (Mutsu Province )|de|Burg_Sōma-Nakamura|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:相馬太田神社 up to rev 98014881
revision.text:
{{精度|date=2014年12月}}<!-- 編集者みずから出典の明記を貼り付けて新規作成するなど無責任。出典も信頼性が低いものばかり --> {{出典の明記|date=2014年12月}} {{神社 |名称 = 相馬太田神社 |画像 = [[File:相馬太田神社.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[福島県]][[南相馬市]][[原町区]]中太田字舘腰143 |ISO = JP-07 |緯度度 = 37|緯度分 = 36|緯度秒 = 39.5 |経度度 = 140|経度分 = 58|経度秒 = 3.1 |ラベル位置 = left |地図 = Japan Fukushima#Japan |位置 = |祭神 = [[天之御中主神]] |社格 = |創建 = [[元亨]]3年（[[1321年]]） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = 妙見太田神社 |札所等 = |例祭 = |神事 = [[相馬野馬追]]（7月23日 - 25日） }}
'''相馬太田神社'''（そうまおおたじんじゃ）は、[[福島県]][[南相馬市]][[原町区]]中太田に立地する[[神社]]。相馬三妙見社の一つ。'''太田神社'''とも呼ばれている。御祭神は天之御中主大神である。
== 概要 == [[承平 (日本)|承平]]年中（[[931年]] - [[937年]]）に[[相馬氏]]の遠祖・[[平将門]]が[[下総国]]猿島郡守屋城に妙見社を創建したことに始まる。
平将門から十二代にあたる[[相馬師常]]が[[源頼朝]]の軍に従い、[[奥州藤原氏]]との合戦での功績により奥州行方郡の地を賜り、同族の[[千葉氏]]から相馬を継ぎ、奥州相馬の初代となった。
その後、[[元亨]]3年（[[1321年]]）[[4月22日]]、[[下総国]]守谷城より相馬孫五郎重胤公が御国換えの際、この地へ移り住み、氏神妙見尊を奉じて宮祠を創建して祀った。
相馬家は[[妙見]]信仰の信望者で、藩主が篤く信仰していた鎮守・妙見を下総から持参し、敷地内に鎮座させた。その妙見堂が相馬太田神社の由来とされている。
この神社は[[相馬中村藩|中村藩]]相馬氏の氏神として代々崇拝されてきた。
== 主な行事 == [[相馬野馬追]]では、各妙見神社で行われる宵乗りの出陣式が行われる。
また、[[大晦日]]の深夜から[[元旦]]の未明にかけて、礼螺吹奏が行われる。
== 重要文化財 == 相馬野馬追が国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されているほかは、南相馬市指定有形民俗文化財に指定されている。 * 相馬野馬追 * 陣太鼓 * 神馬図絵馬 * 鳥毛槍 * 板石塔婆
== 関連項目 == * [[中村城 (陸奥国)]] *[[相馬神社 (札幌市)]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html 相馬太田神社]{{出典無効|date=2014年12月}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=相馬太田神社}} - 特定非営利活動法人福島県ベンチャー・SOHO・テレワーカー共働機構 相双ビューロー * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ 南相馬観光協会 - 神社仏閣]
{{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}}

revision.text:
{{精度|date=2014年12月}}<!-- 編集者みずから出典の明記を貼り付けて新規作成するなど無責任。出典も信頼性が低いものばかり --> {{出典の明記|date=2014年12月}} {{神社 |名称 = 相馬太田神社 |画像 = [[File:相馬太田神社.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[福島県]][[南相馬市]][[原町区]]中太田字舘腰143 |ISO = JP-07 |緯度度 = 37|緯度分 = 36|緯度秒 = 39.5 |経度度 = 140|経度分 = 58|経度秒 = 3.1 |ラベル位置 = left |地図 = Japan Fukushima#Japan |位置 = |祭神 = [[天之御中主神]] |社格 = |創建 = [[元亨]]3年（[[1321年]]） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = 妙見太田神社 |札所等 = |例祭 = |神事 = [[相馬野馬追]]（7月23日 - 25日） }}
'''相馬太田神社'''（そうまおおたじんじゃ）は、[[福島県]][[南相馬市]][[原町区]]中太田に立地する[[神社]]。相馬三妙見社の一つ。'''太田神社'''とも呼ばれている。御祭神は天之御中主大神である。
== 概要 == [[承平 (日本)|承平]]年中（[[931年]] - [[937年]]）に[[相馬氏]]の遠祖・[[平将門]]が[[下総国]]猿島郡守屋城に妙見社を創建したことに始まる。
平将門から十二代にあたる[[相馬師常]]が[[源頼朝]]の軍に従い、[[奥州藤原氏]]との合戦での功績により奥州行方郡の地を賜り、同族の[[千葉氏]]から相馬を継ぎ、奥州相馬の初代となった。
その後、[[元亨]]3年（[[1321年]]）[[4月22日]]、[[下総国]]守谷城より相馬孫五郎重胤公が御国換えの際、この地へ移り住み、氏神妙見尊を奉じて宮祠を創建して祀った。
相馬家は[[妙見]]信仰の信望者で、藩主が篤く信仰していた鎮守・妙見を下総から持参し、敷地内に鎮座させた。その妙見堂が相馬太田神社の由来とされている。
この神社は[[相馬中村藩|中村藩]]相馬氏の氏神として代々崇拝されてきた。
== 主な行事 == [[相馬野馬追]]では、各妙見神社で行われる宵乗りの出陣式が行われる。
また、[[大晦日]]の深夜から[[元旦]]の未明にかけて、礼螺吹奏が行われる。
== 重要文化財 == 相馬野馬追が国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されているほかは、南相馬市指定有形民俗文化財に指定されている。 * 相馬野馬追 * 陣太鼓 * 神馬図絵馬 * 鳥毛槍 * 板石塔婆
== 関連項目 == * [[中村城 (陸奥国)]] *[[相馬神社 (札幌市)]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html 相馬太田神社]{{出典無効|date=2014年12月}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=相馬太田神社}} - 特定非営利活動法人福島県ベンチャー・SOHO・テレワーカー共働機構 相双ビューロー * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ 南相馬観光協会 - 神社仏閣]
{{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=福島県}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|qq=|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]].

== Overview ==
The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|qq=|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|qq=|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]]
* {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

== External Links ==

* {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau

The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|qq=|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|qq=|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|qq=|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]].

== Overview ==
The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|qq=|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|qq=|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]]
* {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]].

== Overview ==
The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]]
* {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]].
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]] * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Somaota-jinja]][[ja:相馬太田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q20045358]] Wikidata name: Sōmaōta-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Accuracy|date=December 2014}} {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Sōma Ōta Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:相馬太田神社.jpg |location = [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta, Azadategoshi 143, [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] |ISO = JP-07 |Latitude degrees = 37|Latitude minutes = 36|Latitude seconds = 39.5 |Longitude degrees = 140|Longitude minutes = 58|Longitude seconds = 3.1 |Label position = left |Map = Japan Fukushima#Japan |deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779}} |Shrine Rank = |consecration_year = Genkyō 3 (1321) |Main hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |Alternate name = Myōken Ōta Shrine |Pilgrimage = |Festival = |Ceremonies = {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} (July 23–25) |location = {{ill|Haramachi-ku|WD=Q24892679}} |coordinates = {{Coord|37.61097222222222|N|140.96752777777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }}
'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]].
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]] * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]].
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]] * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat}} * [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}} * {{wayback|url=http://yumesoso.jp/item.php?itemid=211 |date=20201125113652 |title=Sōma Ōta Shrine}} - Operated by the Non-Profit Fukushima Prefecture Venture, SOHO, Teleworker Cooperation Bureau * [https://web.archive.org/web/20120112162621/http://www.minami-soma.com/kanko/modules/jinja/ Minamisōma Tourism Association - Temples and Shrines]

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]] * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Minamisōma|Minamisōma]], {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]].
== Overview == The shrine traces its origins to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]], established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.
{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during campaigns against the [[:en:Northern Fujiwara|Ōshū Fujiwara]]. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the [[:en:Chiba clan|Chiba clan]], becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.
Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.
The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.
The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the [[:en:Sōma clan|Sōma clan]] and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.
== Major Events == During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.
On [[:en:Ōmisoka|New Year’s Eve]], the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.
== Important Cultural Properties == {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City: * Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi * Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques) * Feathered spears * Stone pagodas == See Also == * [[:en:Sōma Nakamura Castle|Sōma Nakamura Castle]] * {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|en|Minamisōma|WD=Q642094|lt=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|en|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|WD=Q2357104|lt=New Year’s Eve}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas

On {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|WD=Q2357104|lt=New Year’s Eve}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|en|Sōma Nakamura Castle|WD=Q11582470|lt=Sōma Nakamura Castle}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|en|Minamisōma|WD=Q642094|lt=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|en|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|WD=Q2357104|lt=New Year’s Eve}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|en|Sōma Nakamura Castle|WD=Q11582470|lt=Sōma Nakamura Castle}}
* {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Sōma Moritsune|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Sōma Shigetane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|WD=Q2357104|lt=New Year’s Eve}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|en|Sōma Nakamura Castle|WD=Q11582470|lt=Sōma Nakamura Castle}}
* {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

|coordinates = {{Coord|37.61097222222222|N|140.96752777777778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
|architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}
}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|en|Minamisōma|WD=Q642094|lt=Minamisōma|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|en|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan|1=Sōma clan}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Q11582495|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara|1=Northern Fujiwara}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Q11582542|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan|1=Sōma clan}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|WD=Q2357104|lt=New Year’s Eve|1=Ōmisoka}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|en|Sōma Nakamura Castle|WD=Q11582470|lt=Sōma Nakamura Castle|1=Sōma Nakamura Castle}}
* {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

'''Sōma Ōta Shrine''' (相馬太田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Minamisōma|en|Minamisōma|WD=Q642094|lt=Minamisōma|1=Minamisōma}}, {{ill|Haramachi Ward|ja|原町区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haramachi Ward|1=Haramachi-ku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24892679}}, Nakada Ōta. It is one of the three Myōken shrines associated with the Sōma clan and is also known as '''Ōta Shrine'''. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|en|Ame-no-Minakanushi|WD=Q2842779|lt=Ame-no-Minakanushi|1=Ame-no-Minakanushi}}.

== Overview ==
The shrine traces its origins to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} during the Shōhei era (931–937), when {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan|1=Sōma clan}}, established a Myōken shrine at Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province's Sarushima District.

{{ill|Sōma Moritsune|ja|相馬師常|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Moritsune|1=Q11582495|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582495}}, a twelfth-generation descendant of Masakado, received land in Mutsu Province after serving under {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} during campaigns against the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Ōshū Fujiwara|1=Northern Fujiwara}}. Moritsune adopted the Sōma name from a cadet branch of the {{ill|Chiba clan|en|Chiba clan|WD=Q2975237|lt=Chiba clan|1=Chiba clan}}, becoming the first lord of Sōma in Mutsu.

Later, in Genkyō 3 (1321) on April 22, {{ill|Sōma Shigetane|ja|相馬重胤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shigetane|1=Q11582542|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582542}} relocated from Moriyajō in Shimōsa Province to this area. He enshrined the clan deity, Myōken, establishing the current shrine.

The Sōma clan, strong adherents of Myōken faith, brought their revered deity from Shimōsa to this site. The Myōkendō (Myōken Hall) became the basis of what is now Sōma Ōta Shrine.

The shrine has been venerated as the tutelary deity of the {{ill|Sōma clan|en|Sōma clan|WD=Q2975379|lt=Sōma clan|1=Sōma clan}} and served as the clan's ancestral shrine.

== Major Events ==
During the annual {{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}}, nighttime ceremonies for riders are held at various Myōken shrines.

On {{ill|Ōmisoka|en|Ōmisoka|WD=Q2357104|lt=New Year’s Eve|1=Ōmisoka}}, the shrine conducts traditional performances of shell horn blowing, continuing into the early hours of New Year's Day.

== Important Cultural Properties ==
{{ill|Sōma Nomaoi|ja|相馬野馬追|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Nomaoi|qq=}} is designated as a National {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}, and other items are registered as tangible folk cultural properties by Minamisōma City:
* Battle drums used in Sōma Nomaoi
* Painted horse illustrations on ema (votive plaques)
* Feathered spears
* Stone pagodas
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Sōma Nakamura Castle|en|Sōma Nakamura Castle|WD=Q11582470|lt=Sōma Nakamura Castle|1=Sōma Nakamura Castle}}
* {{ill|Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|ja|相馬神社 (札幌市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōma Shrine (Sapporo)|qq=}}

== External Links ==

* [http://www.fukutabi.net/fuku/minamisouma/oota.html Sōma Ōta Shrine] {{Citations needed|date=December 2014}}
